trial_name: RSI — Ketamine vs Etomidate for Tracheal Intubation of Critically Ill Adults (2025)
journal: The New England Journal of Medicine
citation: Casey JD, Seitz KP, Driver BE, et al. Ketamine or etomidate for tracheal intubation of critically ill adults. N Engl J Med. 2025;epublished December 9th
trial_type: RCT — multicenter, pragmatic, parallel-group, unblinded, superiority
one_liner: In critically ill adults undergoing emergency intubation, ketamine did not reduce 28-day in-hospital mortality compared with etomidate but increased peri-intubation cardiovascular collapse.

pico.population: Critically ill adults undergoing emergency tracheal intubation in EDs and ICUs
pico.outcome: Death by day 28 while in hospital

background.existing_knowledge: Emergency tracheal intubation is common in critical care and emergency medicine, with high downstream morbidity and mortality; peri-intubation hypotension/cardiovascular collapse is frequent and prognostically adverse. Etomidate is widely used because of rapid onset and relative haemodynamic stability, but a single dose inhibits adrenal 11β-hydroxylase and suppresses cortisol synthesis for up to ~72 hours, raising concern for worse outcomes (particularly in sepsis). Ketamine is an alternative with analgesia and sympathetic stimulation, but in catecholamine-depleted shock its direct myocardial depressant effects may predominate, plausibly increasing hypotension/collapse.
background.knowledge_gap: Pre-RSI evidence (small randomised trials and heterogeneous meta-analyses) did not establish whether the net clinical effect favours ketamine (avoid adrenal suppression) or etomidate (haemodynamic stability).
background.hypothesis: Ketamine would lower mortality by avoiding etomidate-associated adrenal suppression while maintaining acceptable peri-intubation haemodynamics.

methods.design: Pragmatic, multicentre, parallel-group, investigator-initiated randomised trial; 1:1 allocation; stratified by site with variable block sizes; unblinded.
methods.setting: 14 US sites (6 emergency departments; 8 intensive care units); enrolment April 2022 to August 2025.

inclusion: Adults (≥18 years) undergoing emergency tracheal intubation with an induction agent, where the treating clinician judged both ketamine and etomidate to be reasonable options.
exclusion: Immediate need precluding randomisation without delaying care
Clinician-determined requirement for (or contraindication to) one of the two study drugs

methods.intervention: Ketamine as the induction agent for intubation (intravenous), with clinician-selected dose within a protocol-specified range (1–2 mg/kg). Other peri-intubation care at clinician discretion.
methods.comparison: Etomidate as the induction agent for intubation (intravenous), with clinician-selected dose within a protocol-specified range (0.15–0.30 mg/kg). Other peri-intubation care at clinician discretion.
methods.blinding: Unblinded (treating clinicians, patients, and data collectors); mortality outcome objective; peri-intubation haemodynamic outcomes based on recorded blood pressure/vasopressor use.
methods.sample_size_power: 2308 patients required to detect a 5.2 percentage-point absolute reduction in 28-day in-hospital mortality (30.0% to 24.8%) with 90% power at two-sided α=0.05; inflation for withdrawal yielded a target sample size of 2364.
methods.primary_outcome: Death by day 28 while in hospital.
secondary_outcomes: Cardiovascular collapse between induction and 2 min after intubation (composite: new/increased vasopressor, SBP <65 mmHg, or cardiac arrest)
Time from induction to successful intubation
Lowest systolic BP between induction and 2 min
Cardiac arrest requiring CPR between induction and hospital discharge
Ventricular tachycardia between induction and 2 min (post hoc)
methods.follow_up: Primary endpoint through day 28 while in hospital (censored at discharge); peri-intubation endpoints from induction through 2 minutes after intubation; additional safety/organ support outcomes through 24 hours and to discharge.

headline_bullets: No significant difference in 28-day in-hospital mortality: 29.6% ketamine vs 30.9% etomidate (adjusted RD −0.8 pp; P=0.65).
Ketamine increased peri-intubation cardiovascular collapse (22.1% vs 17.0%; RD 5.1 pp).
Ketamine was associated with a lower nadir systolic BP (86 vs 90 mmHg; median difference −6 mmHg).
No difference in first-pass intubation success (87.4% vs 87.0%).

outcomes_table.outcome: Death by day 28 while in hospital (primary)
outcomes_table.note: ITT; adjusted for site (random effect) with marginal standardisation

outcomes_table.outcome: Cardiovascular collapse between induction and 2 min after intubation (secondary)
outcomes_table.note: Composite: new/increased vasopressor, SBP <65 mmHg, or cardiac arrest

outcomes_table.outcome: Cardiac arrest between induction and 2 min after intubation (component)
Time from induction to successful intubation (sec), median (IQR)
outcomes_table.note: Exploratory; small absolute delay with ketamine

outcomes_table.outcome: Lowest systolic BP between induction and 2 min (mmHg), median (IQR)
outcomes_table.note: Physiological separation consistent with higher collapse rates in ketamine group

outcomes_table.outcome: Systolic BP <80 mmHg between induction and 2 min (post hoc threshold)

outcomes_table.note: No clear between-group difference at 24 hours

outcomes_table.outcome: Cardiac arrest requiring CPR between induction and hospital discharge (safety)
outcomes_table.note: Safety outcome reported through hospital discharge

outcomes_table.outcome: Ventricular tachycardia between induction and 2 min (post hoc safety signal)
outcomes_table.note: Collected after an early event prompted systematic ascertainment; denominators reflect those with data

safety: Cardiac arrest requiring CPR between induction and hospital discharge: 7.5% ketamine vs 5.3% etomidate (RD 2.1 pp; 95% CI 0.0 to 4.2).
Ventricular tachycardia between induction and 2 min (post hoc): 1.0% ketamine vs 0.2% etomidate (RD 0.8 pp; 95% CI 0.1 to 1.5).

domains.notes: Site-stratified randomisation with variable block sizes and allocation concealment via sequential opaque envelopes; randomisation occurred after the clinician decided to intubate and to use an induction agent but before administration.

Unblinded design; mortality is objective, but the secondary composite includes clinician-driven vasopressor initiation (potentially behaviour-sensitive). Objective blood pressure separation (nadir SBP) supports that the haemodynamic signal was not solely definitional.
2367 patients randomised; 2365 included in the primary analysis after post-enrolment withdrawals (very low proportion); primary outcome ascertainment was complete for analysed participants.

High adherence (99.2% received induction medication consistent with assignment); dose separation was clinically substantial (median ketamine 1.6 mg/kg [IQR 1.2–1.9] vs etomidate 0.28 mg/kg [IQR 0.23–0.30]).

Groups were similar in key prognostic variables (e.g., median age 61 vs 60 years; median APACHE II 20 vs 20; sepsis/septic shock 37.1% vs 37.4%; vasopressors within 1 hour before induction 22.0% vs 22.9%), supporting successful randomisation.

internal_validity.verdict: Strong overall: large sample size, robust randomisation with high protocol adherence, and objective primary outcome; the main internal validity caveat is the unblinded nature of haemodynamic co-interventions embedded in the secondary composite.

domains.notes: Broad adult, non-trauma critically ill population across emergency department and ICU settings, including a high prevalence of sepsis/septic shock and respiratory failure; enrolment under emergency research consent processes mirrors real-world urgency.

Pragmatic co-intervention flexibility and dosing ranges aligned with common practice support translation to similar high-resource ED/ICU environments; generalisability is more limited for trauma, prehospital settings, paediatrics, and healthcare systems where etomidate is unavailable or dosing/RSI bundles differ materially. The trial answers a two-agent question (ketamine vs etomidate) and does not directly inform settings where propofol or midazolam are the dominant induction agents.

external_validity.verdict: Good generalisability to adult non-trauma emergency intubations in ED/ICU practice; extrapolation to trauma, prehospital, and markedly different resource contexts should be cautious.

strengths: Large, multicentre pragmatic randomised trial powered for a patient-centred endpoint (28-day in-hospital mortality).
High treatment adherence (99.2%) with clear physiological separation in haemodynamics.
Clinically relevant secondary endpoint (peri-intubation cardiovascular collapse) aligned with prior airway trials and implementation priorities.
limitations: Unblinded design, with potential influence on clinician-triggered components of the cardiovascular collapse composite (vasopressor initiation).
Exclusion of trauma and situations where clinicians required a specific agent may restrict inference in those high-stakes subgroups.
No direct measurement of adrenal function/cortisol response; the mechanistic pathway for etomidate-related harm could not be adjudicated.
Some safety signals (e.g., ventricular tachycardia) were collected after early events prompted additional ascertainment, and should be interpreted as hypothesis-generating.

critical_appraisal.summary: A large, well-conducted pragmatic trial with high internal validity for its primary mortality outcome, though the unblinded design introduces potential bias for clinician-driven secondary haemodynamic endpoints.
strengths: Pragmatic, multicentre design with high protocol adherence and complete follow-up for the primary outcome.
Objective primary outcome (mortality) is robust to the unblinded design.
Clear physiological separation in haemodynamics supports the secondary endpoint findings.
weaknesses: Unblinded design may have influenced clinician-triggered vasopressor use, a key component of the secondary composite outcome.
Exclusion of trauma patients limits generalisability to that population.
Post hoc safety signals require prospective validation.

sections.authors_conclusion: In critically ill adults undergoing emergency tracheal intubation, ketamine did not reduce 28-day in-hospital mortality compared with etomidate but increased peri-intubation cardiovascular collapse.

clinical_bottom_line.verdict: Etomidate is favoured over ketamine for haemodynamic stability in many critically ill intubations, as ketamine offers no mortality benefit and increases peri-intubation cardiovascular collapse.
clinical_bottom_line.applies_to: Adult non-trauma emergency intubations in ED/ICU settings where both agents are reasonable options.
clinical_bottom_line.does_not_mean: Ketamine is contraindicated or that etomidate is risk-free; the absolute mortality was high in both groups, and proactive haemodynamic optimisation is essential regardless of agent.
implementation_caveats: If ketamine is used (e.g., etomidate unavailable or contraindicated), anticipate and mitigate hypotension with early vasopressor readiness and careful dose selection.
Findings do not apply to trauma, prehospital, or paediatric settings.

controversies.topic: Reconciling prior randomised evidence with RSI
controversies.notes: Earlier smaller trials comparing ketamine and etomidate suggested mixed short-term morbidity signals and were not mortality-definitive, leaving persistent uncertainty that RSI was designed to resolve.

Recent meta-analyses reached differing conclusions depending on inclusion criteria and analytic frameworks (frequentist vs Bayesian), reflecting small trial sizes, heterogeneity, and outcome definition variability—limitations addressed by RSI’s scale and pragmatic design.

Large observational analyses have associated etomidate use with worse outcomes in some cohorts, but confounding by indication and practice-pattern effects remain plausible; RSI provides randomised evidence that any net mortality disadvantage of etomidate (relative to ketamine) is not large in the studied population.

The cardiovascular collapse endpoint includes clinician-triggered vasopressor escalation; in an unblinded trial this can embed practice behaviour. RSI mitigates (but does not eliminate) this concern by also demonstrating objective blood pressure separation (median nadir SBP 86 vs 90 mmHg; median difference −6 mmHg).

The most recent RSI-specific guideline from the Society of Critical Care Medicine (pre-RSI) recommended either ketamine or etomidate as reasonable options, emphasising individualisation; RSI provides large randomised evidence favouring etomidate for peri-intubation haemodynamic stability without showing a ketamine mortality advantage, and should inform future updates.

Contemporary Difficult Airway Society guidance (post-RSI publication window) addresses rapid sequence induction and difficult intubation management; RSI’s findings are directly relevant to haemodynamically fragile patients undergoing RSI within these frameworks.

Expert commentary has highlighted that induction-agent choice should prioritise outcomes that matter (mortality and major complications) while acknowledging that peri-intubation haemodynamics are a near-term, modifiable mediator; RSI meaningfully shifts the evidentiary balance towards etomidate in many critically ill intubations.

unresolved_questions: Whether the post hoc safety signal for ventricular tachycardia with ketamine is reproducible.
The role of ketamine vs etomidate in trauma and prehospital intubations.
The impact of induction agent choice in settings where propofol or midazolam are the dominant agents.

numbers_to_remember: 28-day mortality: 29.6% ketamine vs 30.9% etomidate (RD −0.8 pp; P=0.65)
Cardiovascular collapse: 22.1% ketamine vs 17.0% etomidate (RD 5.1 pp)
Nadir SBP: 86 mmHg ketamine vs 90 mmHg etomidate (median difference −6 mmHg)
quick_recall.one_line_takeaway: For emergency intubation in critically ill adults, use etomidate over ketamine when both are options, as ketamine does not improve survival and causes more peri-intubation hypotension.
